name: Subtitle OCR
description: Converts embedded image-based subtitle tracks (VobSub/DVD and PGS/Blu-ray) to external SRT or ASS files using nOCR pattern matching. Pure managed code: no Tesseract and no native libraries, using the ffmpeg your server already ships. The .nocr database format is interchange-compatible with Subtitle Edit, so databases trained or corrected in its GUI drop straight in. A scheduled task scans your Movie and Episode libraries and writes a subtitle file beside each image-based track. OCR text is optionally spell-checked with Hunspell and corrected with Subtitle Edit's OCR fix lists, both fetched on demand.
overview: OCR image-based DVD and Blu-ray subtitles to SRT or ASS

versions.changelog: Italic emphasis follows the whole line, so a stray mid-word italic reads as the run around it while a genuine italic line stays italic. An accented Latin letter foreign to the track's language folds to its base ("Nothing" for "Nothińg"), keeping each language's own accents and leaving a real cast or character name alone; a setting turns it off. Text correction reads a one between two letters as l or I, rejoins a split contraction ("do n't"), closes a space before a comma or period, spaces a leading speaker dash, and folds curly quotes to straight. A cue never ends after the next one begins, and a too-short cue is held to a readable minimum. ASS escapes a literal brace, rounds its timecodes, and places an off-center sign with the full alignment grid. Language and hearing-impaired detection read a cue's words, not its markup, so close languages like Danish and Norwegian separate. Word spacing is calibrated to real disc fonts so run-together words come apart. An optional aggressive mode applies the riskier digit-letter fixes (5/S, 8/B) for a poorly scanned disc.

The bundled Latin database now includes glyph shapes trained on real disc fonts, so far fewer characters read as the unknown box and the text comes out noticeably cleaner. Recognition is faster as well: the matcher caps each comparison at the best match found so far, rounds and looks up candidates more directly, and skips the multi-segment merge for runs that cannot match, all while reading exactly the same characters. Text correction gains several fixes: the two-letter words It, Is, In and If are read with a lowercase l, a zero between letters becomes o (y0u to you), a split double quote rejoins into one, and padded sound-cue brackets tighten ("[ Sighs ]" to "[Sighs]"). A line that opens a sentence is capitalized after spell-check, so a misread at the start of a line resolves against the dictionary rather than freezing into a name it will not correct. When spell-check is enabled but no dictionary is present, the log now says so instead of leaving the text uncorrected in silence.

A run keeps itself off the large object heap by reusing its image buffers instead of allocating one per subtitle, so recognizing a library no longer drives the full garbage collections that interrupt playback. ASS output keeps the color the disc drew its subtitles in, as the track's style, with an override only on the lines that differ; Auto picks ASS for a track that uses more than one color, since that is a distinction SRT cannot carry. Whether a track is light text on a dark outline or the reverse is read off the track itself, per track, so the Invert luma setting is gone. The extracted-files page reprocesses a single item from its row, rather than only the whole log at once. A lowercase l after a speaker label ("STARBUCK: lt's a girl") is read as I, a line that starts a sentence is capitalized using the line before it, and a name is protected from spell-check in both singular and plural, so a cast list saying "Cylons" also protects "Cylon". Multi-segment glyphs (a double quote, an o-slash, a percent sign) are matched on shape, so they read at whatever size the disc drew them.

Runs roughly three times faster: every subtitle track is now pulled out of a file in one pass instead of one full pass per track, and tracks recognize in parallel (see Max parallelism, which leaves half the cores to playback). Recognition picks the best matching glyph rather than the first one the database lists, which fixes words like "cLocK TlcKlNG" at the source and stops italics flickering on and off mid-word, and glyphs the splitter breaks apart (a double quote, an o-slash) now match. A track the database cannot really read is discarded instead of shipped: with only the bundled Latin database, a non-English track is often 10-25 percent unreadable, and those files were being written as if they were fine. Spell-check no longer rewrites names, which cost you ADAMA as ASAMA and TYROL as TYRO; it also sees the whole series cast now, not just an episode's guest stars. The l-to-I fixes only run for English, which stops French losing every elided article. New: hearing-impaired (SDH) tracks are recognized from their own text, the unknown-glyph mark is now a box rather than an asterisk (dialogue uses asterisks), and the extracted-files list moved to its own dashboard page with a Reprocess button.
Loads on every 10.11 server: the plugin is built against 10.11.0, the target ABI it declares, so it no longer requires the assemblies of a later patch release.

The dashboard menu carries both plugin pages: "Subtitle OCR" for the settings and "Extracted Subtitles" for the list of extracted files, which also has a "Reprocess all" button that starts the reprocess task, shows its progress, and reloads the list when it finishes, plus a gear button that opens the settings. The plugin's Settings button opens the settings page.

Fixes 10.11.3.1 stranding you on the extracted-files list: opening it from the plugin settings left no way back to the settings page. The list is now reached from its "Extracted Subtitles" entry in the dashboard menu only, and the settings page no longer links to it.

The list of extracted subtitle files is now a dashboard page of its own, in the main menu, instead of a pop-up inside the plugin settings; the settings button still opens it. The page gained a "Reprocess all" button that starts the reprocess task directly, shows its progress, and reloads the list when it finishes, so re-OCRing everything with the current settings no longer means a trip to Scheduled Tasks.

Output format is now selectable: SRT, ASS, or Auto, which decides per track and writes ASS only when a cue sits away from the bottom of the screen (PGS carries the position), so an ordinary track keeps the universally supported format. OCR text is spell-checked with Hunspell, protecting Title-case proper nouns and words drawn from the item's Jellyfin metadata (title, overview, series, cast and character names) so show and character names survive. Subtitle Edit OCR fix replace lists are applied when present; dictionaries and fix lists download on demand and re-download once older than a configurable age. A track with an undetermined language now has one detected from its OCR text and is named accordingly, and the track title, forced, hearing-impaired, and commentary flags appear in the file name so commentary tracks are identifiable. A hearing-impaired track is recognized from its own text when the source flags nothing, which is the norm for a remuxed disc. New OCR corrections cover misread apostrophes in contractions, mid-word capital X, V, and I, optional ellipsis folding, and bogus italic runs around single characters and punctuation. An unread glyph now costs nothing when matching a word against the dictionary, so "batt□e" resolves to "battle" and a damaged name can be restored from the item's metadata. A new "Reprocess extracted subtitles" task re-OCRs every logged file from its source with the current settings. Each file is probed with a single ffprobe pass rather than two, and only one OCR or reprocess task runs at a time.

Detect image-based subtitles by probing every Movie and Episode with ffprobe, since Jellyfin does not store PGS/VobSub streams in its metadata. A per-file cache skips files that are unchanged and were probed within a configurable window, with a force-rescan option. Word spacing now scales with subtitle line height, fixing spaces inserted between every letter on HD Blu-ray text. New options: extract only selected languages, discard a track whose dropped-event ratio is too high (removing any stale SRT), and a filename tag ({name}.{lang}.OCR.srt, shown as the subtitle title) so the plugin only ever overwrites or deletes its own output. A config-page modal lists every extracted file with a link to its movie or episode. The scheduled task now sits under its own category.

Detect image-based subtitles by probing every Movie and Episode with ffprobe, since Jellyfin does not store PGS/VobSub streams in its metadata. A per-file cache skips files that are unchanged and were probed within a configurable window, with a force-rescan option. Word spacing now scales with subtitle line height, fixing spaces inserted between every letter on HD Blu-ray text. New options: extract only selected languages, discard a track whose dropped-event ratio is too high (removing any stale SRT), and a filename tag ({name}.{lang}.OCR.srt, shown as the subtitle title) so the plugin only ever overwrites or deletes its own output. A config-page modal lists every extracted file with a link to its movie or episode. The scheduled task now sits under its own category.

Initial production release. OCRs embedded image-based subtitles (VobSub/DVD and PGS/Blu-ray) to SRT files using nOCR pattern matching, with Subtitle Edit-compatible .nocr databases. Pure managed code: no Tesseract, no native dependencies. Optionally skips image tracks whose language already has a text subtitle.
